title: Composition and analysis
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to the IB Dance SL Composition and Analysis task. It covers the formulation of a perceptive choreographic intention, the application of choreographic principles and devices, and the effective communication of ideas through movement. The lesson is structured to align with the IB assessment criteria, offering strategies and model examples to help students achieve top-band results.
learningObjectives: Formulate a clear, specific, and perceptive choreographic intention based on a chosen stimulus.
Identify and skilfully apply a range of choreographic principles and devices to structure a dance work.
Effectively communicate a choreographic intention through the coherent organisation of movement.
Analyse the relationship between choreographic intention, process, and product in a formal written analysis.
Understand and apply the language of the IB Dance assessment criteria for Composition and Analysis (Criteria A, B, C).

simpleExplanation.title: Choreography: From Idea to Embodied Intention
simpleExplanation.summary: Dance composition is the art of crafting movement with purpose. It's not just about creating interesting steps; it's about using those steps to explore an idea, tell a story, or evoke an emotion, and then structuring them into a coherent and impactful whole.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of choreographing a dance like writing a formal essay. Your 'choreographic intention' is your thesis statement—the core argument you want to prove. Your 'movement vocabulary' is your evidence and examples. The 'choreographic devices' (like repetition or contrast) are your rhetorical tools, and the 'structure' (like ABA form) is your essay outline. A successful dance, like a successful essay, presents a clear idea and supports it with well-organised, compelling material.
steps: Conceive: Select a stimulus (an image, a poem, a concept) and develop it into a specific and perceptive choreographic intention. Ask 'what is my dance about?' and then 'how will I explore this idea uniquely?'.

Create: Generate movement material through improvisation and structured tasks. Explore the elements of dance—space, time, and energy—to create a rich movement vocabulary that relates to your intention.
Craft: Organise your movement material. Select the strongest motifs, develop them using choreographic devices, and arrange them into a clear structure or form that creates a journey for the audience.

Critique & Refine: Analyse your work as you create it. Film rehearsals and ask for feedback. Does the dance effectively communicate your intention? Be prepared to edit, re-sequence, and refine your choices to strengthen the final work.

flashcards.back: The purpose behind the dance; the central idea, theme, or concept that the choreographer aims to explore and communicate to the audience. A top-band intention is 'perceptive' and 'specific'.

The starting point or inspiration for a dance. It can be visual (a painting), auditory (music, a soundscape), ideational (a concept like 'chaos'), kinaesthetic (a specific movement quality), or literary (a poem).
A single, distinct movement or short movement phrase that is repeated and developed throughout a dance. It acts as a signature theme for the work.
The way in which movement material, particularly a motif, is changed, varied, or manipulated. This can be done by altering its space, time, or energy qualities.
Repeating a movement or phrase exactly as it was first performed. Used for emphasis and to create familiarity for the audience.
The use of opposing elements to create interest and highlight key ideas. This can be contrast in energy (sharp vs. sustained), space (high vs. low), or dancers (solo vs. group).
The point of highest tension or energy in a dance. A successful climax is a culmination of the dance's developed themes and is structurally significant.
A common choreographic structure. Section A presents the main theme, Section B introduces contrasting material, and Section A returns in some form (exactly the same or slightly varied).
A choreographic structure that tells a story, with a clear beginning, middle, and end. It often involves characters and a sequence of events.
A choreographic device where dancers perform the same phrase one after another, with a slight time delay, like a 'round' in music. It creates a ripple effect.
A choreographic device where two or more dancers perform the exact same movement at the exact same time. It creates a sense of unity and power.
A choreographic device where a movement phrase is performed backwards, from the end to the beginning. It is a sophisticated method of development.

faq.q: Can my stimulus be a piece of music?
faq.a: Yes, but a top-level intention must go beyond simply 'dancing to the music'. Analyse the music: what qualities, emotions, or structures does it possess? Your intention should be about exploring those specific elements, for example, 'to embody the metric tension between the 3/4 violin melody and the 4/4 cello rhythm in the chosen score'.

faq.q: What is the difference between a motif and a phrase?
faq.a: A motif is the shortest coherent unit of movement that has a distinct identity, like a single key word or idea. A phrase is a longer sequence of connected movements that feels like a complete sentence or thought. A phrase can contain one or more motifs.

faq.q: Do I have to perform in my own composition?
faq.a: No. You can choreograph on other dancers. The assessment is of your choreographic skill, not your performance ability. However, you are responsible for directing your dancers to ensure they perform the work with the clarity and quality needed to communicate your intention effectively.

faq.q: How much should I talk about my 'process' in the 800-word analysis?
faq.a: The analysis is not a diary of your creative journey ('first I felt stuck, then I had an idea...'). Instead, it is a formal analysis of the final product. You should discuss your choices and justify them in relation to your intention. You can mention challenges if it helps to explain why a certain choice was made, framing it analytically (e.g., 'An initial challenge was..., which was resolved by developing the motif through... to better communicate...').

sections.content: Welcome to the core practical component of the IB Dance course: Composition and Analysis. This task is your opportunity to function as a dance artist, moving from an initial idea to a fully realised choreographic work. It assesses not only your ability to create a dance but also your capacity to analyse your own creative choices with intellectual rigour. Mastering these skills is essential for achieving excellence in the internal assessment.
Deconstructing the Assessment: Composition and Analysis

This internal assessment task requires you to choreograph a dance of 3-5 minutes for either a solo performer or a group of 2-5 dancers. You will submit a video recording of the final dance, accompanied by a written analysis of no more than 800 words. The task is worth 35% of your final SL grade and is assessed internally by your teacher and moderated externally by the IB. It is marked against three criteria.

items: Criterion B: Application of choreographic principles (10 marks)
Criterion C: Communication of choreographic intention (10 marks)

sections.content: This criterion assesses the quality of your core idea. A top-band response requires a 'clear, specific and perceptive' intention. This means moving beyond a general topic. For example, 'a dance about grief' is too broad. A perceptive intention would specify the aspect of grief being explored, such as 'the cyclical nature of grief, exploring the tension between private sorrow and public composure'. This provides a much stronger foundation for generating specific movement and structuring your dance.

sections.question: A student's initial idea is to create a dance inspired by the painting 'The Persistence of Memory' by Salvador Dalí. Refine this into a 'clear, specific and perceptive' choreographic intention.
sections.solution:
An adequate intention might be: 'My intention is to create a dance about melting clocks and the surrealism in Dalí's painting.'

A perceptive, top-band intention would be: 'My choreographic intention is to embody the distortion of time and reality as depicted in Dalí's 'The Persistence of Memory'. The work will contrast fluid, melting qualities of movement with rigid, clock-like gestures to explore the psychological state of navigating a dreamscape where the familiar laws of physics no longer apply. The dance aims to evoke a sense of unease and temporal dislocation in the audience, rather than simply illustrating the painting.'

sections.content: Criterion B: Application of Choreographic Principles

This criterion assesses your 'choreographic craft'—how you use the tools of dance making to build your work. This includes your selection and development of movement vocabulary, your use of the elements of dance (space, time, energy), and your structuring of the work into a coherent whole. Examiners look for the 'skilful' and 'imaginative' application of principles like motif, development, contrast, and form (e.g., ABA, narrative, episodic).

In your written analysis, do not just list the devices you used. You must justify your choices. Explain *why* you used a specific device and *how* it serves to realise your choreographic intention. For example, instead of saying 'I used canon', say 'To communicate the spreading of an idea, a gestural motif was performed in a three-part canon, visually representing the ripple effect of a single thought moving through a collective consciousness.'

sections.question: For the choreographic intention 'to explore the cyclical nature of grief', provide an example of how you would analyse your use of 'motif and development' in your 800-word analysis.
sections.solution: A model paragraph for the analysis could be: 'The central theme of cyclical grief is embodied in a recurring floor-based motif introduced at the beginning of the work. This motif consists of a slow, contracted curl into the foetal position, followed by a strained, arching push away from the floor, which ultimately fails and collapses back into the initial curl. This movement sequence serves as the core emotional anchor of the piece. Throughout the dance, this motif is developed to reflect different stages of the grieving process. In the central section, for instance, the tempo of the motif is dramatically increased and performed with sharp, percussive energy, communicating a sense of anger and resistance. Later, it is developed through diminution, becoming a small, repetitive hand gesture of clenching and unclenching, signifying an internalised, persistent ache. This skilful development of a single motif ensures thematic coherence while allowing for the nuanced expression of a complex emotional journey, directly serving the choreographic intention.'

sections.content: Criterion C: Communication of Choreographic Intention

This is the 'so what?' criterion. It assesses how successfully the final dance (as seen on video) communicates the intention you laid out in Criterion A, using the tools you described in Criterion B. A top-band work is 'highly effective' and 'coherent'. The choreography, structure, and performance must all work together to create a powerful and legible artistic statement. The whole should be greater than the sum of its parts.

items: Coherence is key: Every section, phrase, and gesture should feel like it belongs and contributes to the overall intention.
Performance quality matters: The dancer(s) must perform the choreography with technical clarity and appropriate expression to make the choreographer's vision clear.
Consider the audience: How will your choices be 'read' by an observer? Film your work from the audience's perspective to check for clarity.
Effective structuring: The beginning should establish the world of the dance, the middle should develop the ideas, and the ending should provide a sense of resolution or poignant finality.

sections.content: Your 800-word analysis is crucial for helping the examiner understand how the choices you made in the choreography are meant to communicate your intention. Use precise timestamps from your video to refer to specific moments, linking the visible movement directly to your conceptual goals. This demonstrates a reflective and analytical creative process.
